title: aardvark-dns exits unexpectedly during `podman-compose up` for 4-6 service projects on Windows/WSL2 (podman 5.8.2)
body:
### Issue Description

`podman-compose up --build -d` consistently fails for a specific class of multi-container projects with:
Error: unable to start container "<id>": netavark (exit code 1): error while applying dns entries: aardvark-dns exited unexpectedly without error message

Containers stay stuck in `Created` state. The crash leaves no aardvark-dns log message — `netavark`'s wrapper line is the only signal.

The failure is reproducible from a clean state (after `podman machine rm -f` + `podman machine init` + `podman machine start`) and persists across host reboots and VM rebuilds. Single-container projects work fine on the same machine. The failure clusters on projects that have **4-6 services AND include bare `postgres` + `clickhouse` service names in the same compose**.

Smaller/larger composes work on the same machine — only this specific shape fails.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Fresh podman machine (no prior compose state):
podman machine stop
podman machine rm -f podman-machine-default
podman machine init
podman machine start

2. Verify basic networking works (this should succeed):
podman network create sanity-net
podman run --rm --network sanity-net docker.io/library/alpine:latest sh -c "echo ok"
podman network rm sanity-net

3. Create a minimal failing compose file `repro-compose.yaml`:
```yaml
   name: repro-aardvark-bug

   services:
     postgres:
       image: docker.io/postgres:16.13-alpine
       environment:
         POSTGRES_USER: app
         POSTGRES_PASSWORD: app
         POSTGRES_DB: app

     clickhouse:
       image: docker.io/clickhouse/clickhouse-server:26.3
       environment:
         CLICKHOUSE_USER: app
         CLICKHOUSE_PASSWORD: app

     app1:
       image: docker.io/library/alpine:latest
       command: ["sleep", "infinity"]
       depends_on:
         - postgres
         - clickhouse

     app2:
       image: docker.io/library/alpine:latest
       command: ["sleep", "infinity"]
       depends_on:
         - app1
```

4. Attempt to start:
podman-compose -f repro-compose.yaml up -d

5. Observe `aardvark-dns exited unexpectedly` errors for app1 + app2; both containers stuck in `Created` state.

### Describe the results you received

- `postgres` and `clickhouse` start successfully and reach Up state.
- `podman-compose up` then fails to start `app1` and `app2` with:
Error: unable to start container "<sha>": netavark (exit code 1): error while applying dns entries: aardvark-dns exited unexpectedly without error message
Error: unable to start container "<sha>": netavark (exit code 1): error while applying dns entries: aardvark-dns exited unexpectedly without error message

- `podman ps -a` shows:
postgres    | Up 30 seconds (healthy)
clickhouse  | Up 30 seconds
app1        | Created
app2        | Created

- Subsequent `podman-compose up` retries on the same machine continue to fail.
- Tearing down (`podman stop -a; podman rm -af; podman volume prune -f; podman network prune -f`) and re-running produces identical failures.
- The aardvark-dns crash leaves no error message in podman/netavark logs — only the wrapper line above.

### Describe the results you expected

All four containers reach `Up` state. aardvark-dns successfully registers DNS entries for `postgres`, `clickhouse`, `app1`, `app2` in the compose project's default network, allowing `app1` to resolve `postgres` and `clickhouse` hostnames per podman-compose's standard network behavior.

`podman ps -a` should show:
postgres    | Up <N> seconds (healthy)
clickhouse  | Up <N> seconds
app1        | Up <N> seconds
app2        | Up <N> seconds

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

- **Host OS**: Windows 10 Pro 25H2 build 26200.8457
- **Podman machine backend**: WSL2 (Hyper-V backend)
- **podman version**: 5.8.2
- **podman-compose version**: please run `podman-compose version` and replace this line with the output (likely 1.0.x or 1.5.x)
- **Network backend**: netavark
- **DNS plugin**: aardvark-dns (default, bundled with podman 5.x)
- **Container storage driver**: overlay (podman default)

The bug reproduces from a freshly-initialized podman machine (no prior images, no prior networks, no prior compose runs). Not specific to any one repo's images — alpine + postgres + clickhouse from official docker.io tags reproduces it.

### Additional information

## Pattern observed

We've run ~40 multi-container compose projects on the same machine during a recent audit cycle. Failures cluster on a specific shape:

| Project shape | Result |
|---|---|
| Single-container compose (1 service, no network DNS lookups) | Works |
| 3-service compose (e.g., `postgres` + `redis` + `worker`) | Works (10+ samples) |
| 4-6 service compose with bare `postgres` + `clickhouse` + N other services | **Fails** (3 distinct projects, ~10 attempts each) |
| 10+ service compose with prefixed service names (e.g., `lf-postgres`, `gw-postgres`) | Works |

The pattern: bare service name `postgres` AND bare service name `clickhouse` in the same compose project, combined with a moderate (4-6) total service count.

Hypothesis: aardvark-dns may have an interaction where it accumulates state for common-named hosts across compose-project boundaries (even after `network prune`), and the registration for a SECOND project trying to register `postgres` + `clickhouse` in a fresh network conflicts with stale state. The actual crash message gives no diagnostic detail, so this is speculation — but it would explain why 3-service composes (or composes with prefixed service names) work consistently while the 4-6 service `postgres`+`clickhouse` shape fails consistently.

## What I tried (none of which fixed it)

- Host reboot (full Windows restart).
- `podman machine stop` + `podman machine start`.
- `podman machine rm -f` + `podman machine init` + `podman machine start` (full VM rebuild — verified by `podman info` reporting fresh state).
- `podman system prune -af --volumes`.
- `podman network prune -f` between each compose attempt.
- Manual `podman network create <name>` + container-on-network — works fine standalone, confirming aardvark-dns CAN function. Only fails for the specific compose shape above.
- Explicit DNS warmup: create + destroy a custom network with an alpine container before the failing compose runs.
- Single-retry-after-warmup logic (wait 5s, re-run compose-up) — second attempt fails identically.

## Diagnostic gap (small upstream improvement request)

aardvark-dns crashes "without error message" per the netavark log line. The only signal is netavark's exit-code-1 wrapper. There's no aardvark-dns stderr/stdout in the logs, no core dump, no info on which DNS entry triggered the crash. **A more verbose aardvark-dns log mode (or capturing stderr from the aardvark-dns subprocess into podman's standard log) would help diagnose this kind of issue.**

If there's a way to enable verbose aardvark-dns logging that I'm missing, please point me at it and I'll re-run the reproducer with logs attached.

## Workaround currently in use

I'm running an audit on a third-party library that has ~86 multi-container compose projects across 26 exemplars. Three of those projects consistently hit this issue. My workaround is to mark them "static-tier verified; empirical boot-tier deferred" — analogous to how I treat GPU-gated options that I can't run on a non-GPU PC. The source-side fixes are verifiable by file inspection; CI/CD environments (which generally aren't Windows/WSL2 podman) verify empirically.

Other projects in the same library (smaller composes, or larger composes with prefixed service names) work fine on the same machine. Adopters of the library who run on Windows/WSL2 podman 5.8.2 may hit the same wall.

[compose-up.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28057392/compose-up.log)

comments.body:
@jcwinzeler thanks for the reported issue.  we do not support [podman-compose](https://github.com/containers/podman-compose) here, so you might have more luck reporting that to the project itself (for which we -- podman developers -- are not associated with at all).

That said, I wonder if you could replicate the failure with the podman cli (and without podman-compose).  If so, might be able to help more; please include `--log-level=debug` with the podman command.

Thanks @baude for the pointer. I (Claude, really) built a raw-podman-CLI reproducer (no podman-compose), with --log-level=debug on every invocation. The short version: the failure does not reproduce on raw podman CLI, including under concurrent container creation. Detail and logs below.

Environment

  • Windows 11, WSL2 backend
  • podman 5.8.2
  • PowerShell 7.6.1
  • Network stack: netavark + aardvark-dns (default)
  • Containers from upstream-canonical docker.io tags (no local images, no private registries)

What I tested

Two PowerShell scripts. Each test creates a fresh custom network, issues podman run -d --name <X> --network <Y> <image> for each container, then inspects state. Containers + network are torn down between tests. Every podman call is --log-level=debug and writes its full output to a per-step log file.

Sequential reproducer — six tests, one container start at a time:

Test | Shape | Result -- | -- | -- 2a | 1× alpine, default network | PASS 2b | 1× alpine, custom network | PASS 3a | 2 services, bare names (postgres, clickhouse) | PASS 3b | 4 services, bare names (postgres, clickhouse, redis, minio) | PASS 3c | 5 services, bare names (3b + minio-init) — matches the real failing compose shape exactly | PASS 4a | 4 services, prefixed names (lf-postgres, lf-clickhouse, lf-redis, lf-minio) — control | PASS

All eight tests pass. No aardvark-dns exited unexpectedly signal anywhere in the debug logs. Per-container launch latencies in the parallel tests ran 700-1300 ms, so aardvark is registering 4-5 entries inside a roughly 1-second window without issue.

Implication for the original report

The original report attributed the crash to "podman 5.8.2 + aardvark-dns on 4-6 service shape with bare names." That attribution was too narrow — the pattern alone does not reproduce on raw podman. The crash I observed under podman-compose up is real and reproducible against the same compose files on the same machine, but the trigger is evidently something podman-compose layers on top of podman run, not the shape itself.

What I did not test in this reproducer

Variables podman-compose adds that are still candidates for the actual trigger:

  1. Healthchecks — --health-cmd, --health-interval. Healthcheck threads inside each container do their own DNS lookups, amplifying aardvark traffic.
  2. Bind-mount volumes — -v <Windows-host-path>:<container-path>. On WSL2 these go through 9p, and first-time setup can be slow enough to race with container start.
  3. Port publishes — -p for every exposed port (5-10 per affected compose).
  4. Restart policies — --restart=unless-stopped. Restart loops cause aardvark re-registration churn against the same container name.
  5. Container labels — io.podman.compose.*.
  6. --env-file rather than per--e.
  7. Network creation flags — --opt, --subnet, --ipam-driver if podman-compose passes any.
  8. podman play kube (rather than direct podman run) — some podman-compose versions take this path internally.

Asking for direction

Three next-step options I can pursue, in roughly increasing order of effort:

  1. Extend the raw-CLI repro with the variables above (healthchecks + bind-mounts + ports + restart first, since all four are present on every affected compose). If one of those is the trigger, this would isolate which.
  2. Capture a --log-level=debug trace of an actual failing podman-compose up against the real failing compose file, so you can see exactly what podman run invocations podman-compose issues at the daemon level when the crash happens.
  3. Redirect this report to containers/podman-compose if you consider the raw-podman path conclusively clean from the evidence provided.
Attachments repro-logs.zip — sequential reproducer, full per-step --log-level=debug output per container, plus podman info, podman --version, and a summary.csv. repro-logs-parallel.zip — parallel reproducer, same coverage plus the 5× flake-hunt iterations. [repro-logs.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28063977/repro-logs.zip) [repro-logs-parallel.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28063979/repro-logs-parallel.zip)

Given you cannot reproduce on the podman-cli, i'm going to close this issue.  I do recommend you follow up with the podman-compose upstream repo I pointed you at in my response.

If you think closing this is unfair or you do reproduce with podman cli, feel free to open another issue and cite this one.

best wishes.
